candidate_access_open_ungated: Yes, portal-level access verified; exact file-level LGP download URL not yet verified
candidate_citation: FAO & IIASA. 2025. Global Agro-ecological Zoning version 5 (GAEZ v5) Model Documentation.
candidate_description: Open-access FAO/IIASA agro-ecological assessment platform using updated soils and climate inputs, including future climates based on bias-corrected ISIMIP3b CMIP6 forcing under AR6-used models.
candidate_variables: Length of growing period and related agro-climatic indicators not yet individually verified from portal; future climate impact layers documented at system level

candidate_processing_summary: FAO states GAEZ v5 integrates AgERA5 historical observations with CMIP6 future climate forcing and provides open-access spatial indicators through the geospatial platform.
objective_better_statement: GAEZ v5 is better than the legacy Ericksen et al. 2011 LGP threshold layer for modernization review because FAO states it uses updated climate inputs derived from bias-corrected CMIP6 forcing used in the IPCC AR6 and provides an open-access platform with improved spatial resolution; however, an exact open future LGP threshold-flip layer matching the CRI construct is not yet verified.
objective_better_criteria: newer climate-model era; closer alignment to authoritative practice; improved spatial resolution; open-access stewardship
limitations: Exact future LGP threshold-flip layer not yet verified; file-level ungated download URL not yet verified; resolution statements differ between FAO overview and model documentation; license not confirmed from reviewed sources.
evidence_type: official steward page + official model documentation
adversarial_findings: Core challenge: reviewed sources confirm modern CMIP6-based GAEZ v5 platform, but do not yet prove that an exact open LGP greater-than-120 to less-than-120 future threshold-flip layer is directly downloadable and like-for-like with the CRI row. Resolution is also internally ambiguous: FAO overview cites 30 arc-seconds for key outputs, while model documentation cites several terabytes at 5 arc-minutes.
challenge_disposition: Do not replace yet. Keep as leading candidate family for follow-up, but mark row Clarify until exact variable-level access and comparability are confirmed.
reviewer_notes: WorldClim CMIP6 remains useful as climate backbone candidate, but not demonstrated here as direct like-for-like replacement for an LGP threshold-flip hazard layer.

current_function_in_cri: Estimate average number of drought events per year per pixel using three consecutive months with less than 50 percent of average precipitation.
candidate_dataset_name: CHIRPS v3 3-monthly precipitation for SPI-style drought derivation
candidate_steward: Climate Hazards Center, UC Santa Barbara
candidate_access_open_ungated: Yes, official direct open directory is exposed on steward infrastructure
candidate_license: Public domain (for CHIRPS v3 Beta, as stated on official page)
candidate_citation: Funk et al. 2026 Scientific Data paper; CHIRPS v3 official steward page; CHIRPS v3 direct data access directory.
candidate_description: CHIRPS v3 combines a high-resolution climatology, thermal infrared geostationary satellite observations, and station observations into a 0.05 degree precipitation record spanning 1981 to near-real time.
candidate_variables: Precipitation totals available at daily to multi-month intervals, including rolling 3-month totals suitable for SPI-style drought derivation; not a precomputed CRI event-frequency layer
candidate_temporal_resolution: daily, pentad, dekad, monthly, annual, and rolling multi-month products; reviewed candidate uses 3-monthly
candidate_baseline_period: CHIRPS time series begins in 1981; exact SPI climatology baseline for a CRI-equivalent derivation remains unverified
candidate_processing_summary: Official README and open methods paper state CHIRPS v3 combines climatology, thermal infrared satellite observations, and station blending; v3 adds variance-preserving precipitation estimation, more station sources, gauge-undercatch correction, and ERA5-based filling for missing data, with rolling multi-month products exposed directly.
objective_better_statement: CHIRPS v3 is better than the legacy Ericksen et al. 2011 SPI lineage for modernization review because the official steward page states it is a public-domain, 0.05 degree, 40+ year precipitation product with improved station density and variance performance relative to CHIRPS v2, and the official data directory exposes open 3-month products that are closer to the current drought-event logic; however, it is still an input family rather than a precomputed CRI-ready SPI event layer.
objective_better_criteria: more current maintained product; finer spatial resolution; direct open access; explicit stewardship improvements documented by source
limitations: Not a precomputed SPI or drought-event layer; current CRI uses a specific three-month drought-event rule rather than standard SPI alone; spatial coverage is 60N to 60S rather than fully global; candidate remains historical / near-present and does not solve future-horizon alignment by itself.
evidence_type: official steward page + direct access directory + official README + open methods paper
adversarial_findings: Core challenge: CHIRPS v3 is clearly open and modern as a precipitation input family, but it is not a direct like-for-like replacement for the CRI SPI event-frequency layer. It also does not cover poleward areas outside 60N to 60S and does not by itself address future hazard alignment.
challenge_disposition: Do not replace yet. Keep as leading open precipitation candidate family for drought-derivation follow-up, but mark row Clarify until global fit and CRI-equivalent derivation are demonstrated.
reviewer_notes: Saved evidence bundle now includes the official README and open Scientific Data paper; CRI-equivalent event derivation still needs explicit method confirmation.

current_function_in_cri: Identify areas where average maximum daily temperature shifts from less than 30 C to greater than 30 C during the primary growing season, masked where length of growing period is greater than 40 days.
candidate_dataset_name: WorldClim CMIP6 future climate monthly maximum temperature
candidate_access_open_ungated: Yes, direct file URL is exposed on official WorldClim page; file retrieval was not independently completed in this review pass
candidate_license: CMIP6 terms of use linked from official page; dataset-specific license not separately captured in this review pass
candidate_citation: WorldClim future climate data page; WCRP CMIP6 overview page; Fick and Hijmans 2017 for WorldClim baseline context if needed
candidate_description: Official WorldClim page provides downscaled CMIP6 monthly future climate projections, including monthly average maximum temperature for multiple GCMs, SSPs, future periods, and resolutions.
candidate_variables: Monthly average maximum temperature (tx); not a precomputed growing-season threshold-flip indicator
candidate_models: Nine GCMs at 30 seconds; WorldClim overview page also states 23 GCMs across available resolutions
candidate_bias_adjustment: Downscaling and calibration (bias correction) done with WorldClim v2.1 baseline climate
candidate_downscaling: Statistically downscaled future climate grids at multiple resolutions
candidate_processing_summary: Monthly CMIP6 tx GeoTIFF files exposed directly on official WorldClim page at multiple resolutions and future periods.
objective_better_statement: WorldClim CMIP6 monthly maximum temperature is better than the legacy Ericksen et al. 2011 growing-season heat threshold layer for modernization review because the official WorldClim page states it provides downscaled CMIP6 future projections, uses bias correction against WorldClim v2.1 baseline climate, offers direct open file links, and explicitly notes the older downscaled CMIP5 data are obsolete; however, it does not directly provide the CRI growing-season threshold-flip indicator.
objective_better_criteria: newer climate-model era; open direct download links; explicit CMIP6 basis; obsolete CMIP5 predecessor acknowledged by steward; finer spatial resolution
limitations: Current CRI row is a growing-season threshold-flip indicator, while candidate provides monthly average maximum temperature only; growing-season mask and threshold-flip derivation still need to be built separately; dataset-specific license not captured; direct file retrieval not independently completed in this pass.
evidence_type: official steward page + official WCRP CMIP6 page
adversarial_findings: Core challenge: candidate is clearly modern and open, but not directly like-for-like. It provides monthly average maximum temperature rather than a ready-made 'average maximum daily temperature flips from <30 C to >30 C during the primary growing season' layer. Additional derivation and growing-season logic are required, so direct replacement claim would overstate evidence.
challenge_disposition: Do not replace yet. Keep as strong candidate family for deriving a superior open heat indicator, but mark row Clarify until exact CRI-equivalent derivation path is demonstrated.
reviewer_notes: CHELSA future climate products may also be worth review later, but WorldClim was easier to verify as open and directly downloadable from official pages in this pass.
